story:
Fermin trailed Tailah to the woods behind the schoolhouse, nerves prickling as her laughter pulled him past the tree line. Their mothers would be furious—his for stealing a freshly washed sheet, hers if he lost her daughter—but Tailah had promised an adventure. She found a clearing ringed by rocks and low branches, perfect for a makeshift fort. Together they draped the sheet over a limb, Tailah insisting they could pitch it without putting holes in it and provoking his mother’s wrath. She teased him about witches—old stories adults told to keep kids out of the woods—and reminded him, as always, that she planned to be one someday.

While they hunted for weights, Tailah reappeared holding a deer skull, mostly clean, one antler snapped to a jagged nub. She set it on a corner of the sheet like a sentinel and christened it Hieronymus. Fermin’s stomach turned, but her grin and the way she lit up softened his protest. Soon they were tucked under the sheet. Tailah kept stealing glances at the skull; Fermin spread his cardigan to keep dirt off their knees and tried to pretend the fort felt safe.

A salamander darted across the edge of their hideout. Tailah lunged, fingers closing too fast; the creature slipped free—almost. In the scramble, she caught a tiny leg, and it tore. The salamander wriggled to the shadows, a thin limb still in her grip, twitching as the last impulses faded. Tailah stared, more stunned and fascinated than apologetic. Fermin recoiled, accusing her of cruelty. She insisted it wasn’t on purpose, but her cool curiosity chilled him. Repulsed and hurt by how far she seemed to have drifted into the macabre, he snatched his cardigan and marched away.

He should have been back at the schoolhouse in fifteen minutes. Instead, lost in thoughts of the old, kinder Tailah and his mother’s looming scolding, he wandered. A root snagged his foot and sent him sprawling. When he stood, the trees had thickened, the canopy knitted tight. The woods hushed as if the world were holding its breath. The light sank to dusk-blue, and every shadow sharpened. He turned in circles; every path grew darker and more tangled. Panic quickened his steps. Branches clawed his face and sleeves. Spider silk webbed his hair. Breath ragged, he barreled blindly until a low limb struck his throat and dropped him hard to the leaf litter.

“Tailah?” he croaked. “Tailah!”

Leaves rustled to his left. Then behind him. Then to the right. Then ahead. Small mounds trembled and rose, the wind absent and yet the forest moving. The leaf piles lifted into figures—shawls woven from dead foliage, crowns braided from twigs and vine—closing in from all sides. They pointed. They moaned. Fermin could not run. He screamed Tailah’s name again and again.

Years later—five since Fermin vanished—Tailah jolted awake, lungs burning, the old recurring dream of being buried alive gripping her harder than ever. This time the fear felt threaded with a voice, like hearing Fermin call from very far away. By the window, a strip of water-treated birch fluttered. In a hand she didn’t recognize, it bore three words: Come to the woods.
